responses.strategic_rationale: The project must reconcile a tight budget with the need for high‑quality, longitudinal parasomnia data, while maintaining participant safety and a naturalistic environment. Core tensions arise between staffing costs and continuous coverage, recruitment speed and event yield, data openness and privacy, and manual annotation burden versus automated triage. The selected levers target these conflicts by redefining operational staffing, refining event capture strategy, formalizing data governance, advancing a tiered triage algorithm, optimizing recruitment pathways, and structuring publication plans. Together they provide a balanced framework that aligns financial constraints, scientific rigor, and ethical safeguards, ensuring the unit can achieve its validation, phenotyping, and dissemination goals within the 36‑month horizon.

levers.name: Staffing Coverage Model
levers.consequences: Changing the staffing model alters payroll, coverage reliability, and response times to captured events. A more rigid schedule may reduce overtime costs but increase technician fatigue, potentially affecting annotation quality. Conversely, a flexible model can improve coverage but may raise training and supervision expenses.
options: Maintain three full‑time technicians rotating 8‑hour shifts with a dedicated on‑call senior technician to cover all eight suites, ensuring continuous coverage and rapid response to events.
Implement a hybrid staffing model that pairs part‑time night technicians with an on‑call senior technician, allowing flexible coverage while reducing payroll costs.
Outsource night coverage to a specialized sleep monitoring service that provides trained technicians and real‑time data integration, freeing internal staff for research tasks.
levers.review_lever: Balancing cost against continuous coverage; options ignore potential technician burnout and skill drift during extended shifts.

levers.name: Event Capture Frequency
levers.consequences: Adjusting the frequency of enhanced PSG nights changes data resolution and participant burden. More frequent PSGs increase event capture probability but raise costs and may reduce participant compliance. Fewer PSGs lower expenses but risk missing rare parasomnia episodes.
options: Increase the frequency of enhanced PSG nights to every third night, thereby capturing more high‑resolution events while maintaining participant comfort.
Deploy adaptive sensor thresholds that trigger full PSG only when preliminary data indicates a high probability of an event, optimizing resource use.
Introduce a pre‑admission home PSG to pre‑screen participants and schedule enhanced nights based on detected event frequency, reducing unnecessary PSG nights.
levers.review_lever: Maximizing event yield versus participant burden; options do not address long‑term compliance or data storage load.

levers.name: Data Sharing Governance
levers.consequences: The chosen data sharing policy determines openness, privacy compliance, and potential collaborations. A restrictive model limits external reuse but safeguards participant confidentiality, while a more open model may attract collaborators but increases regulatory scrutiny.
options: Share only de‑identified physiological data with controlled access, requiring collaborators to sign data use agreements that prohibit re‑identification.
Provide a public repository for raw sensor data with strict privacy filters that remove any video or audio containing identifiable features.
Offer a subscription‑based data access model to collaborators with usage agreements, generating revenue while maintaining control over data distribution.
levers.review_lever: Openness versus privacy; options overlook legal audit requirements and future regulatory changes.

levers.name: Event‑Triage Algorithm
levers.consequences: The algorithmic approach influences development time, validation rigor, and interpretability. A rule‑based system is quick to implement but may miss subtle patterns, whereas a machine‑learning model can capture complex signals but requires extensive labeled data and may be opaque to reviewers.
options: Build a rule‑based scoring system that applies thresholded sensor metrics to flag probable parasomnia episodes, enabling rapid triage.
Train a supervised machine‑learning classifier on annotated events, using cross‑validation to optimize sensitivity and specificity.
Combine rule‑based and ML outputs in an ensemble that weighs each method’s confidence, improving overall detection performance.
levers.review_lever: Speed versus rigor; options fail to consider integration with annotation workflow or explainability for reviewers.

levers.name: Recruitment‑Yield Optimization
levers.consequences: Modifying recruitment strategies affects enrollment numbers, budget, and participant diversity. Expanding referral sources can increase sample size but may introduce selection bias, while higher compensation accelerates enrollment but raises costs.
options: Expand recruitment to additional regional sleep clinics, broadening the participant pool and increasing event capture opportunities.
Offer higher per‑night compensation to attract participants, thereby improving enrollment speed while raising budgetary demands.
Implement a pre‑admission screening video call to triage eligibility, reducing screening time and ensuring only suitable candidates are admitted.
levers.review_lever: Enrollment speed versus cost; options miss potential selection bias or participant attrition.

levers.name: Publication Strategy
levers.consequences: The dissemination plan shapes the speed of knowledge transfer, peer‑review rigor, and institutional visibility. Prioritizing high‑impact journals may delay publication but enhance credibility, whereas open‑access venues accelerate reach but may attract less scrutiny.
options: Prioritize methods validation papers in high‑impact sleep journals, establishing credibility and attracting future collaborators.
Publish longitudinal phenotyping results in open‑access venues, maximizing reach among clinicians and researchers worldwide.
Release a preprint series to accelerate community feedback, allowing rapid iteration before formal peer review.
levers.review_lever: Speed of dissemination versus rigor; options neglect embargo periods or institutional review board constraints.

responses.strategic_rationale: Strategic rationale: The residential parasomnia unit hinges on tight coordination across safety, utilization, ethics, data handling, technology, and finance. By targeting safety protocols, suite efficiency, ethics cadence, annotation workflow, equipment refresh, and funding alignment, the levers directly address the core tensions of participant risk, data quality, regulatory compliance, operational cost, and budget sustainability. These levers collectively ensure that the facility can capture high‑fidelity longitudinal data while maintaining participant trust, staff well‑being, and fiscal discipline, thereby positioning the project to meet its scientific and publication milestones within the constrained three‑year window.

levers.name: Participant Safety Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever establishes robust safety oversight, reducing incident risk and improving participant confidence. However, increased staffing and monitoring may raise operational costs and require additional training. Real‑time logging also generates data that must be securely stored, adding IT overhead.
options: Implement a tiered safety monitoring system that assigns a dedicated safety officer to each suite, conducts nightly risk assessments, and logs incidents in real time.

Deploy an automated environmental alert network that triggers immediate staff response when motion, temperature, or door status deviates from predefined thresholds, while maintaining participant privacy.
Introduce a peer‑review safety audit where participants and staff jointly evaluate safety measures after each stay, feeding feedback into continuous improvement.
levers.review_lever: Balancing rigorous safety with participant comfort and operational feasibility; options assume staff availability and participant willingness to engage in audits, missing potential delays from regulatory approvals for automated alert systems.

levers.name: Suite Utilization Efficiency
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever increases suite occupancy, reducing idle costs and accelerating data collection. However, tighter scheduling may compress staff shift planning and increase risk of over‑extension. Short‑stay packages may dilute event yield per participant.
options: Schedule staggered admission windows that align participant stay durations with suite turnover, maximizing occupancy while preserving rest periods for staff.
Introduce a dynamic booking algorithm that reallocates suites in real time based on event frequency and technician availability, ensuring high utilization.
Offer flexible short‑stay packages for participants with low event probability, allowing them to occupy suites for fewer nights without compromising data quality.
levers.review_lever: Maximizing occupancy versus maintaining data quality and staff well‑being; options overlook potential regulatory constraints on participant stay lengths and the impact of rapid turnover on infection control protocols.

levers.name: Ethics Compliance Cadence
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever ensures continuous oversight, reducing risk of ethical breaches and enhancing participant trust. Yet, increased reporting may burden staff and slow decision cycles. Frequent reviews could also lead to conservative data handling that limits analytic flexibility.
options: Conduct quarterly ethics board reviews that incorporate interim data summaries, participant feedback, and risk assessments to preemptively address emerging concerns.
Implement a rolling ethics audit where a dedicated compliance officer submits monthly progress reports and corrective action plans to the board.
Establish a real‑time ethics dashboard that aggregates key metrics and flags deviations, enabling the board to intervene proactively between formal meetings.
levers.review_lever: Maintaining rigorous ethics oversight while preserving research agility; options assume board availability and may not account for delays in obtaining approvals for new monitoring technologies.

levers.name: Data Annotation Workflow
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever streamlines annotation, improving throughput and consistency. However, added automation and audits may increase upfront development costs and require training. Consensus algorithms could introduce bias if not properly validated.
options: Adopt a two‑tier annotation pipeline where technicians perform initial labeling, followed by automated quality checks that flag inconsistencies for senior review.
Deploy a collaborative annotation platform that allows multiple raters to annotate simultaneously, with built‑in consensus algorithms to resolve disagreements.
Introduce a periodic annotation audit where a random sample of scored nights is re‑evaluated by an external expert to calibrate inter‑rater reliability.
levers.review_lever: Balancing speed of annotation with accuracy and reliability; options do not address potential data privacy concerns arising from external expert involvement.

levers.name: Technology Refresh Cycle
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever maintains high‑quality data capture and reduces downtime, supporting research goals. Yet, frequent upgrades may strain budget and require staff retraining. Vendor‑agnostic policy could lead to compatibility issues across data pipelines.
options: Schedule bi‑annual hardware refreshes that replace aging sensors with newer models, ensuring signal fidelity and reducing maintenance downtime.
Implement a phased upgrade strategy that pilots emerging sensor technologies in a subset of suites before full deployment, minimizing disruption.
Adopt a vendor‑agnostic procurement policy that allows rapid acquisition of alternative equipment when performance thresholds fall below defined benchmarks.
levers.review_lever: Ensuring cutting‑edge data quality versus budget constraints and system stability; options overlook the need for long‑term data format compatibility and potential regulatory approvals for new equipment.

levers.name: Funding Milestone Alignment
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever aligns financial resources with project progress, reducing risk of overspending and ensuring timely completion. However, strict milestone gating may delay necessary expenditures and create administrative overhead. Rolling models may reduce flexibility for unexpected opportunities.
options: Tie budget releases to quarterly deliverables, requiring formal progress reports and milestone verification before disbursement.
Introduce a rolling funding model that allocates a fixed percentage of total funds each month, with adjustments based on real‑time cost tracking.
Establish a contingency reserve that automatically triggers additional funding when key performance indicators fall below predefined thresholds.
levers.review_lever: Financial control versus operational flexibility; options assume predictable cost structures and may not account for inflation or currency fluctuations affecting budget.

responses.strategic_rationale: The residential parasomnia unit must reconcile the need for high‑fidelity, longitudinal data with limited financial and human resources, while maintaining participant safety and regulatory compliance. The selected levers target the most critical decision points: participant engagement, staffing efficiency, data integrity, environmental control, fiscal resilience, and publication strategy. By addressing these levers, the project can navigate the core tensions between data yield, cost, operational feasibility, and scientific credibility, ensuring that the unit remains sustainable, produces robust phenotyping results, and meets its publication and funding milestones.

levers.name: Participant Retention Incentive Model
levers.consequences: Increasing compensation or flexibility raises average stay duration, potentially boosting event capture rates but also raising per‑participant cost and logistical complexity. Longer stays may improve data richness but risk higher dropout if compensation or flexibility is insufficient. The balance between financial sustainability and participant satisfaction must be monitored closely.
options: Offer a tiered compensation structure that increases with length of stay, encouraging participants to remain for full monitoring period.
Provide personalized sleep education sessions during the stay, creating perceived value that motivates continued participation.
Implement a flexible scheduling system that allows participants to adjust nightly activities, reducing perceived burden and fostering loyalty.
levers.review_lever: Core tension between maximizing data yield and controlling participant cost; options overlook potential regulatory limits on compensation and the impact of extended stays on staffing workload.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to keep participants engaged throughout stay

levers.name: Night Shift Staffing Flexibility
levers.consequences: Adjusting staffing models can reduce overtime expenses and improve coverage reliability, but may increase training overhead and affect consistency of annotation quality. Cross‑training may dilute expertise, while part‑time hires could introduce variability in adherence to protocols. Staffing flexibility must be weighed against the risk of reduced technician morale.
options: Deploy a rotating 12‑hour shift schedule that aligns technician availability with peak event‑response windows.
Introduce a cross‑training program enabling technicians to cover both monitoring and annotation duties during night shifts.
Utilize a part‑time night‑shift specialist pool sourced from local universities to supplement core staff during high‑volume periods.
levers.review_lever: Tension between cost efficiency and maintaining high annotation fidelity; options fail to address potential legal constraints on part‑time labor and the risk of reduced technician morale with rotating schedules.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to schedule staff for night coverage

levers.name: Data Annotation Quality Assurance
levers.consequences: Strengthening QA improves inter‑rater reliability and data validity, but adds time and personnel costs. Quarterly audits may catch drift early but require additional administrative effort. Senior adjudication can resolve conflicts but may delay data availability for analysis.
options: Institute a double‑blind scoring protocol where two independent raters annotate each event without knowledge of each other's results.
Deploy a periodic inter‑rater reliability audit that recalibrates scoring thresholds every quarter.
Introduce a senior reviewer who adjudicates discrepancies after initial scoring, ensuring final consensus.
levers.review_lever: Core tension between data quality and turnaround speed; options do not consider the impact of increased annotation time on participant throughput or the potential for reviewer fatigue.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — how to ensure annotation accuracy

levers.name: Environmental Noise Mitigation
levers.consequences: Reducing environmental noise enhances signal‑to‑noise ratio, improving event detection accuracy, but requires upfront material costs and ongoing maintenance. Real‑time monitoring can prompt immediate corrective action but may increase technician workload. Noise mitigation must preserve the domestic feel to avoid suppressing naturalistic behavior.
options: Install acoustic panels on all suite walls and ceilings to absorb low‑frequency vibrations from neighboring traffic.
Deploy white‑noise generators in each suite synchronized with ambient sound levels to mask sporadic external noises.
Implement a real‑time noise‑monitoring system that alerts technicians when decibel thresholds exceed acceptable limits.
levers.review_lever: Tension between improving recording fidelity and budget constraints; options ignore the potential for noise mitigation measures to alter the domestic feel of the suites, possibly affecting participant naturalistic behavior.
levers.lever_classification: product — how to reduce ambient noise affecting recordings

levers.name: Funding Contingency Allocation
levers.consequences: A dedicated contingency fund safeguards against budget overruns, but ties up capital that could be used for early recruitment. Milestone‑based release aligns funds with progress, potentially delaying availability during crises. In‑kind support reduces cash burn but may introduce dependency on external partners.
options: Reserve 10% of the total budget as a contingency fund to cover unforeseen equipment repairs or staffing shortages.
Allocate contingency funds on a milestone basis, releasing reserves only after meeting predefined operational targets.
Partner with a local university to secure in‑kind support, reducing the need for a large cash contingency.
levers.review_lever: Core tension between financial risk mitigation and operational flexibility; options overlook the risk of delayed fund release impacting critical mid‑project interventions.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to allocate reserves for budget overruns

levers.name: Publication Dissemination Timing
levers.consequences: Early publication can boost reputation and funding prospects but may expose incomplete data, risking credibility. Waiting until full data set is available ensures robust findings but delays dissemination and potential impact. Conference abstracts keep stakeholders informed while awaiting full journal submissions.
options: Publish preliminary methods validation results within 12 months of project start to secure early visibility and attract collaborators.
Delay first publication until after the 24‑month data collection phase to ensure comprehensive longitudinal findings.
Release a series of conference abstracts during the project to maintain engagement while awaiting full journal submissions.
levers.review_lever: Tension between rapid visibility and data completeness; options fail to account for the impact of early publication on participant confidentiality agreements and the need for data de‑identification before public release.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_classification`: a brief phrase with the category and what the lever decides.
   - Format: "category — what this lever decides".
   - Categories: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - Examples: "governance — who oversees the review process", "methodology — which data collection approach to use", "execution — how to sequence the rollout phases".

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.
